thread.title: Council approves plan for big-rig dealership next to Eastside neighborhood
thread.title_full: Council approves plan for big-rig dealership next to Eastside neighborhood

author: Shari Biediger
title: Council approves plan for big-rig dealership next to Eastside neighborhood
text:
The owners of a big-rig sales business got the green light from city council on Thursday to build a new dealership adjacent to a San Antonio neighborhood.
Councilman Jalen McKee-Rodriguez, whose Eastside district is where the dealership will be built, expressed regret before voting to approve it.
\u201cAs I promised to my neighbors, I would like to convey the spirit of sadness and lost hope that they have expressed as I make this motion to approve these nasty little items,\u201d he said.\xa0
Three rezoning and land use requests from Doggett Freightliner of Texas and Arkansas made it on the council agenda Thursday.\xa0
The first was to extend the San Antonio city limits by annexing 16.3 acres of Doggett\u2019s total 35-acre property at Weichold Road and Interstate 10 East.
The second and third items approved by council resulted in an ordinance amending the IH-10 East Corridor Perimeter Plan, changing the land classification from \u201curban living\u201d to \u201ccommunity commercial,\u201d and a rezoning that allows for oversized vehicles sales, service and storage.\xa0\xa0
Paloma residents have fought the Doggett plan since the start, organizing with an apartment developer to oppose the dealership they said would make the area unsightly and possibly unsafe. They had hoped for the land to be developed into a mixed-use retail space that would provide amenities to the neighborhood.
Three-year Paloma resident said her biggest concern is the increased traffic the dealership will create in and around the neighborhood. She was disappointed in the council\u2019s decision.
\u201cThe thing is, we\u2019re an active neighborhood,\u201d she said. \u201cThey\u2019re not backing up to another business. They\u2019re backing up to a functioning neighborhood and the city just let us down.\u201d
A sign protesting the rezoning of a lot next to Paloma neighborhood for Doggett Freightliner hangs from a wire-fence inside the neighborhood. Credit: Brenda Baz\xe1n / San Antonio Report The council\u2019s approval came after months of talks and delays, and at least one surprise end-run.
In June, the Zoning Commission sided with the dozens of Paloma residents and an apartment developer who asked the city to deny the rezoning next to the subdivision.\xa0
When the commission\u2019s recommendation went to council in October, McKee-Rodriguez had told Doggett\u2019s representative, attorney Ken Brown, he would postpone it to later in the month.
Instead, to Brown\u2019s surprise \u2014\xa0and that of his colleagues on the council \u2014 the councilman called for a vote. \u201cI had to make a decision that was going to benefit my constituents,\u201d he said.
After Brown asked for a delay and a long discussion on the dais, the council voted to postpone the items to Nov. 21.\xa0
McKee-Rodriguez stepped in to mediate an agreement between the two sides, Brown said, that eventually eased the concerns of Paloma homeowners who had fought the dealership since last summer. The last of their meetings occurred the night before council voted.
\u201cMuch of the meeting was coming to terms with the fact that a budding neighborhood with a lot of promise is now placed in the position that much of my district is in,\u201d McKee-Rodriguez said.\xa0
\u201cThey will exist in a food desert,\u201d he added. \u201cThey will not have access to the retail restaurants or many other amenities that make neighborhoods feel like home. Their neighborhood will become an industrial hub.\u201d
The Paloma neighborhood is seen behind the lot Doggett Freightliner has requested to rezone. Credit: Brenda Baz\xe1n / San Antonio Report In the end, Paloma residents reluctantly agreed that the dealership would be preferable to Doggett\u2019s alternate proposed use \u2014 a parking lot and storage for 18-wheelers.\xa0
\u201cWe worked with the residents to get as much out of the dealership as possible,\u201d McKee-Rodriguez said.\xa0
The company has agreed not to allow its trucks on Weichold Road near the neighborhood, to increase buffers on the property lines from 15 feet to 50 feet and 25 feet and build a masonry fence.\xa0
The dealership\u2019s operating hours will be limited to 13 hours on weekdays and nine hours on Saturdays.\xa0
The building will be situated at least 200 feet from abutting homes and the tree canopy within the buffer area will be maintained, according to McKee-Rodriguez. No liquid fuel storage is allowed on the site.\xa0
In addition, Doggett agreed to contribute $50,000 for improvements to a park space in the Paloma neighborhood.\xa0
\u201cLastly, by annexing the property, we will have an opportunity to hold them accountable in ways that we would not if they remained outside of city limits,\u201d McKee-Rodriguez said.\xa0
Brown said Doggett would likely break ground on the project next year and open the dealership in 2026.\xa0The company\u2019s existing dealership near Converse will be closed.
Doggett also owns another 24-acre parcel nearby, closer to Loop 1604, that Brown said will not be developed for any other purposes until after the dealership is built.
Scott Hardwick\u2019s house backs up to the future dealership site. The decision to grant Doggett\u2019s request baffles him.
\u201cI never thought I would be a guy that was a \u2018not in my backyard\u2019 type of person, but going through this, I became that,\u201d Hardwick said.
\u201cI didn\u2019t know anything about planning, zoning \u2026 before doing this,\u201d he added. \u201cBut I don\u2019t know how the city invested all this time, effort, taxpayer money, into this corridor plan, and then completely made a decision that doesn\u2019t concern that plan at all, completely ignored their own zoning board that decided \u2026 not to support this [and] completely ignored all the residents.\u201d
